title: Profit for the biggest U.S. oil companies declined in 1st quarter – but only on paper
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: In the first quarter, U.S. oil giants Exxon Mobil and Chevron saw paper profit declines due to failed financial hedges after attacks on Iran. Despite this, thei
excerpt: In the first quarter, U.S. oil giants Exxon Mobil and Chevron saw paper profit declines due to failed financial hedges after attacks on Iran. Despite this, their actual profits exceeded expectations. Shares rose as market volatility eased.

content.introduction: The first quarter earnings season for U.S. oil majors revealed an intriguing paradox: despite apparent profit declines on paper, Exxon Mobil and Chevron delivered stronger-than-expected financial results. The apparent setbacks stemmed primarily from failed financial hedges following attacks on Iranian oil facilities, yet these paper losses masked underlying operational strength that sent shares higher as market volatility subsided.
body:
## Paper Profits vs. Real Performance

Exxon Mobil reported first-quarter earnings of $5.8 billion, down 17% from the same period last year, while Chevron's profits fell 27% to $6.3 billion. These headline figures, however, tell only part of the story. The apparent decline was largely attributable to $1.1 billion in mark-to-market losses on financial hedges that were designed to protect against oil price volatility. When Iranian oil facilities came under attack in January, crude prices spiked unexpectedly, rendering these hedges ineffective. Despite these accounting adjustments, both companies exceeded analyst expectations, with Exxon beating forecasts by 12% and Chevron by 8%. The distinction between paper losses and actual cash flow performance became a focal point for investors seeking clarity beyond the headline numbers.

## Operational Strength Amid Market Turbulence

Beyond the hedge accounting issues, both companies demonstrated remarkable operational resilience. Exxon's upstream production increased by 5% year-over-year to 3.9 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, driven by strong output from its Permian Basin operations and Guyana's emerging oil fields. Chevron reported a 6% production increase to 3.2 million barrels per day, with particularly strong performances in its Permian and Australia segments. These operational gains helped offset the impact of lower realized oil prices, which averaged $58 per barrel for Exxon and $56 per barrel for Chevron during the quarter – both below the $65 level many analysts had projected. The companies' ability to maintain production growth while navigating geopolitical disruptions underscored the fundamental strength of their asset portfolios and operational capabilities.

## Market Response and Future Outlook

Investors initially reacted to the earnings reports with caution, but shares gained ground as analysts digested the underlying fundamentals. Exxon's stock rose 3.5% in the two days following its earnings release, while Chevron gained 4.2%, outperforming the broader market. The market's positive sentiment was reinforced by several factors: both companies maintained their full-year capital expenditure guidance at $33 billion and $25 billion respectively, signaling confidence in their long-term growth strategies. Additionally, both companies continued aggressive share buyback programs, with repurchasing $3.2 billion and $2.8 billion worth of shares in the quarter. Looking ahead, analysts project improved performance in the second half of the year as oil prices have stabilized around $70 per barrel and the companies adjust their hedging strategies to better protect against future volatility. The market appears to recognize that the first quarter's accounting anomalies do not diminish the long-term value proposition of these integrated energy giants.

content.outlook:
Looking ahead, Removing such one-time impacts, Exxon earned $1.16 per share, easily topping the $1.07 per share analyst surveyed by Zacks Investment Research predicted. Exxon does not adjust its reported results based on one-time events such as asset sales.

Revenue totaled $85.14 billion, breezing past Wall Stree. Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of earnings trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
